content.text: When a platform team supports five product squads all making LLM calls, they quickly discover that each squad has independently wired up retry logic, cost tracking, error handling, and model selection — each slightly differently. An
is a thin adapter layer your team owns and distributes as an internal package (e.g.

) that sits between application code and the raw vendor SDKs. It provides one consistent interface regardless of whether calls go to Anthropic, OpenAI, or Azure OpenAI, and enforces governance controls — token budgets, cost visibility, retry policies, and observability — once, centrally, for every caller.

Without a shared wrapper, the vendor SDK contract leaks into every service. When Anthropic adds a new field or changes a stop-reason string, all consumers must update independently. When the company switches from OpenAI to Anthropic for a workload class, each service must be migrated separately. And when the CFO asks how much the LLM bill is by team and use-case, there is no answer because nobody instrumented the calls.

These are not theoretical problems. They surface within weeks of an enterprise deploying LLMs at scale. The wrapper pattern resolves all of them at the cost of one well-maintained internal library. It is the same reason companies have internal HTTP clients, internal database connectors, and internal logging libraries — vendor lock-in and cross-cutting concerns are solved once at the infrastructure layer, not dozens of times in application code.
Swap Anthropic for OpenAI — or add a fallback — by changing one adapter, not every service.
Every call passes through the wrapper, which calculates cost from a pricing table and emits a metric or writes to a cost ledger. Finance dashboards follow automatically.
Exponential backoff with jitter is implemented once with the correct rate-limit semantics for each vendor (including Anthropic's
Per-team and per-request token limits prevent runaway spend. A team's daily budget is checked before the call is dispatched — not discovered on the invoice.
OpenTelemetry spans covering model, latency, token counts, and cost are emitted for every call without application code touching the tracing API.
Route classification tasks to a cheaper model automatically, without callers hardcoding model names.
The platform team must keep the wrapper compatible with vendor SDK upgrades. This is a real ongoing cost, particularly when vendors release breaking SDK changes.
When a vendor releases a new capability (e.g. extended thinking, computer use), the wrapper must expose it before any consumer can use it. Wrappers that are slow to update become blockers.
Provider-specific parameters that have no analog in the unified interface — Anthropic's
— must be either exposed as provider-specific overrides or excluded. Neither option is fully clean.

Wrapping streaming correctly — propagating the generator and maintaining the OTel span across chunk boundaries — is significantly harder than wrapping batch calls. Many internal wrappers skip streaming support initially, which then forces workarounds in latency-sensitive consumers.
A bug in the wrapper affects every consumer simultaneously. The wrapper must have its own thorough test suite and a semantic-versioned release process.
The wrapper is a thin layer of Python (or TypeScript) classes that conform to a common
) interface. A factory function instantiates the correct adapter based on a
argument. The adapter translates the unified call signature into the vendor SDK call and translates the vendor response back into a shared
Cross-cutting concerns — retry, cost, token budgeting, OTel spans — are implemented as decorators or middleware that wrap the adapter's

method. They execute regardless of which provider adapter is active. This separation means the retry policy is tested once and works for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Azure OpenAI without any adapter-specific logic.
Cost tracking uses a static pricing table — a dictionary mapping model name to input and output price per million tokens. After each successful call, the wrapper reads
from the response, multiplies by the price table, and emits the cost as a metric tagged with the calling team's identifier.
Token budgeting adds a pre-call check against a Redis key holding the team's remaining daily token allowance. If
plus an estimated prompt size would exceed the budget, the call is rejected with a
before any network request is made. After a successful call, the actual token count is subtracted from the remaining budget.

attrs.alt: Internal LLM SDK wrapper layered architecture: Application Code at top, Internal SDK in the middle with Unified LLMClient, Retry/Backoff, Cost Tracking, Token Budgeting, and Model Routing sub-components, Provider Adapters (Anthropic, OpenAI, Azure) below, Vendor APIs at the bottom, with a cross-cutting concerns panel on the left covering OTel spans, Redis budget, pricing table, model routing rules, and retry policy.

content.text: The architecture has four horizontal layers.

without knowing which provider is active. The SDK's five functional sub-components — unified interface, retry/backoff, cost tracking, token budgeting, and model routing — each run in sequence for every call. The SDK delegates to a
(Anthropic, OpenAI, or Azure OpenAI) which translates the call into the vendor SDK format. Finally, the adapter calls the
. The dashed panel on the left represents cross-cutting concerns that are active across all layers simultaneously.
More than two teams are independently making LLM calls in the same organisation.
Cost visibility is required — finance or leadership wants spend attributed by team or use-case.
The stack uses more than one LLM provider (e.g. Anthropic for reasoning tasks, a cheaper model for classification).
Compliance or governance requires audit trails of what was called, when, and by whom.
A single service is sufficient in the short term — but plan the wrapper before the second service is built to avoid retrofitting instrumentation later.

Avoid building a wrapper for a solo-developer project or a prototype where the overhead of maintaining a package outweighs the benefit. The break-even point is roughly two to three services making LLM calls independently.
Implement one thin adapter per vendor:
Build the retry decorator around the adapter's
call. Catch the vendor rate-limit exception and sleep for the value in
(or use exponential backoff with jitter if the header is absent).
Add the cost calculator. Build a pricing table dictionary (model → input price/MTok, output price/MTok). After a successful call, compute
. Emit via your metrics library or append to an internal ledger.
Add token budgeting. Before each call, check the team's remaining budget in Redis (
). Reject calls that would overdraw. After the call, reconcile the actual usage.
Wrap the call in an OpenTelemetry span. Record
Package as an internal PyPI package. Pin the vendor SDK versions in
(compatible-release) to allow patch updates but not breaking minor bumps.
Core LLMClient with Protocol Interface, Retry, and Cost Tracking

# acme_llm_client/budgeting.py
# pip install redis

import redis
from .client import LLMProvider, LLMRequest, LLMResponse

class TokenBudgetExceededError(Exception):
    """Raised when a call would exceed the team's remaining daily budget."""
    pass

class BudgetedProvider:
    """Wraps a provider with per-team daily token budget enforcement via Redis."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        provider: LLMProvider,
        redis_client: redis.Redis,
        team_id: str,
        daily_token_limit: int = 1_000_000,
    ):
        self._provider = provider
        self._redis = redis_client
        self._team_id = team_id
        self._daily_limit = daily_token_limit

    def _budget_key(self) -> str:
        from datetime import date
        return f"llm:budget:{self._team_id}:{date.today().isoformat()}"

    def complete(self, request: LLMRequest) -> LLMResponse:
        key = self._budget_key()
        # Initialise key with TTL on first access of the day (86400 seconds = 24h)
        if not self._redis.exists(key):
            self._redis.set(key, self._daily_limit, ex=86400)

        remaining = int(self._redis.get(key) or 0)
        # Reject if request.max_tokens alone would overdraw
        if request.max_tokens > remaining:
            raise TokenBudgetExceededError(
                f"Team {self._team_id}: requested {request.max_tokens} tokens, "
                f"only {remaining} remain today."
            )

        # Make the call
        response = self._provider.complete(request)

        # Deduct actual tokens used
        actual_used = response.input_tokens + response.output_tokens
        self._redis.decrby(key, actual_used)
        return response

A fintech platform team supports eight product squads, each calling Claude for tasks ranging from fraud explanation to document parsing. Before the wrapper existed, every squad was on a different minor version of the Anthropic SDK, none were passing
prompts consistently, and the monthly LLM invoice could only be attributed to "engineering" as a whole.

in six weeks: two weeks for the Anthropic and OpenAI adapters plus retry logic, two weeks for Redis budgeting and the OTel instrumentation, and two weeks for internal rollout (migrating two squads per sprint). By version 1.2.0, all eight squads were on the wrapper.
The immediate business outcomes: cost attribution by
was visible in Grafana the same week. The fraud squad's experimental agent — which had been accidentally calling
in a classification loop — was rerouted to
was set, reducing that squad's weekly LLM spend by 80%. When Anthropic introduced a breaking change to its
field names in a minor SDK bump, one fix in
resolved it for all eight squads simultaneously.

Batch calls return a single response object, which is straightforward to wrap. Streaming calls return a generator. A wrapper that collects the generator into a string before returning loses the latency benefit of streaming entirely. The correct approach is to yield chunks from the adapter's
method and close the OTel span and record cost only after the stream is exhausted — which requires a context-manager-style wrapper or a callback.

header on HTTP 429 responses indicating exactly how many seconds to wait. A wrapper that uses fixed exponential backoff without reading this header will either wait too long (wasting latency) or retry too aggressively (worsening the quota situation). Always read
Hardcoding Model Names in Application Code

. When a new model is released, routing can be updated in one place in the wrapper without touching every caller. Hardcoded model names are a soft coupling to the vendor's versioning scheme and a common source of drift.
broadly will silently retry on programming errors —
— that should propagate immediately. The retry catch must be limited to
A Redis budget key without an expiry never resets. If the TTL set call fails (e.g. due to a race condition on the
gap), the team's budget could be permanently exhausted. Use
(set only if not exists, with expiry) to make the initialisation atomic.
Application code pins to a minor version (
). Any interface change that could break callers is a major version bump.
Pin the vendor SDK in the wrapper's dependencies.
to allow patch updates but block minor upgrades that could change response shapes until the wrapper is tested against them.
An adapter should translate — not add business logic. If a caller needs to do tool use, they do it through the adapter's standard messages format. The adapter never inspects tool call content.
Expose an escape hatch for provider-specific parameters.
that is passed through to the underlying SDK call. This avoids blocking callers who need an Anthropic-only parameter without adding it to the unified interface.
Test adapters against the real SDK in CI.
The wrapper's own test suite should include at least one integration test per adapter that calls the live API. Run these on a schedule, not on every PR push, to catch SDK drift early.
Document the task_type routing table publicly.
values exist and which model they map to. Keep a changelog so squads know when routing changes.

The wrapper adds negligible overhead to each call. The OTel span creation and Redis budget check together take under one millisecond in a local datacenter, compared to 200–800 ms for a typical LLM round-trip. Cost calculation is purely arithmetic with a dictionary lookup.
At scale (millions of calls per day), the Redis budget check becomes a potential bottleneck if the Redis cluster is undersized or the key-access pattern creates hot keys. Mitigation options:
naturally spreads across Redis cluster nodes if team IDs are diverse.
Cache remaining budget locally in the calling process for a short TTL (e.g. 5 seconds) to reduce Redis round-trips for high-frequency callers.
If Redis is unavailable, fail open (allow the call) and log a warning rather than blocking all LLM traffic. Budget enforcement is a governance control, not a critical-path dependency.
Model routing is stateless and pure-Python — it adds zero network hops. The routing table can be loaded from a YAML file and refreshed via a background thread if rules change frequently.
The official SDK for Anthropic's Messages API. The
exception hierarchy is the primary error surface the adapter must map from.
Also used for Azure OpenAI via the
subclass. The error hierarchy mirrors Anthropic's structurally.
The CNCF standard for distributed tracing. Spans created in the wrapper propagate through the caller's trace context automatically if
The standard Python Redis client. For budget key atomicity, use

An open-source library that provides a unified interface over 100+ LLM providers. Consider using it as the foundation for your adapters rather than writing raw SDK calls, particularly if your provider matrix is large. Trade-off: LiteLLM is another external dependency with its own versioning.
Both handle building and publishing to internal PyPI.
is significantly faster for dependency resolution in CI, which matters when the wrapper is installed as a transitive dependency across many services.

The internal wrapper has the highest upfront implementation cost but the most control. LiteLLM as a library is a strong foundation if the provider matrix is large and you don't want to write raw SDK calls. The LiteLLM proxy adds a network hop but provides a vendor-agnostic HTTP gateway with dashboards out of the box. LangChain's LLM abstraction works well if the stack is already built on LangChain but is a poor standalone choice due to its large transitive dependency footprint.
The wrapper should read API keys from environment variables (
) — which the vendor SDKs already do. The wrapper should never accept or log key values directly.

OTel spans should record token counts and cost — not the prompt content or completion text. Recording prompt text in traces risks leaking PII or proprietary business data to your observability platform.
Budget enforcement is governance, not security.
The Redis budget check can be bypassed by a caller who constructs the vendor SDK directly. Treat budget limits as a cost control for trusted internal callers, not a security boundary.
A retry policy that retries up to 4 times can multiply traffic by 4x during an outage. Cap max retries at 3–4 and add a circuit breaker (e.g.
) to stop retrying entirely if the error rate is too high.
If the vendor SDK returns an empty content list (e.g. a filtered response), the adapter should raise a clear

The architecture diagram above shows the full four-layer stack (Application Code → Internal SDK → Provider Adapters → Vendor APIs) with the cross-cutting concerns band on the left. The key visual insight is that all five governance components — unified interface, retry, cost tracking, token budgeting, model routing — sit at the Internal SDK layer and are therefore activated for every call, regardless of which Provider Adapter handles the dispatch. This is the entire value proposition of the pattern: write the governance logic once, enforce it everywhere.
Think of the internal LLM SDK wrapper as a
customs checkpoint at an international airport

. Every flight (LLM call) from every gate (application service) must pass through the same checkpoint before leaving the terminal (your infrastructure). At the checkpoint, a consistent set of controls runs: identity verification (team ID / auth), luggage scanning (token budget check), import duties (cost tracking), and a travel log (OTel span). Which airline operates the specific flight (Anthropic, OpenAI, Azure) is irrelevant to the checkpoint — it processes every flight the same way.

Application services are the travellers. They don't interact with the checkpoint directly; they just get on their flight. The platform team runs the checkpoint. New governance rules (e.g. a new cost policy) are implemented at the checkpoint and immediately apply to all gates. No individual airline (vendor SDK) needs to be reconfigured.

An internal LLM SDK wrapper is a thin adapter package that decouples application code from vendor SDKs, enforcing cost tracking, retry logic, token budgets, model routing, and observability once for all callers.
The core design is: a shared
Protocol, one thin adapter per vendor, and a facade (
) that composes retry, cost, budgeting, and OTel as layers around the adapter.
from rate-limit responses and use exponential backoff with jitter — not fixed sleeps — when the header is absent.
Token budgeting via Redis requires atomic
initialisation; fail open (allow the call) if Redis is unavailable to avoid blocking LLM traffic on a budget-enforcement failure.
, not model names. The ModelRouter translates task type to (provider, model). Model upgrades are applied in one place.
Streaming, provider-specific parameters, and circuit breaking are the three features most commonly deferred and most commonly regretted. Plan for them in v1 even if they ship in v2.
Distribute as a semantically versioned internal PyPI package with pinned vendor SDK dependencies. Every consumer pins to a minor version of the wrapper, not to the vendor SDK directly.
